coredata.dc:description: Carbon dots (CDs) have emerged as a promising class of nanomaterials for addressing critical environmental challenges. Their unique optical, chemical, and electronic properties enable various applications, including microplastic detection, gas sensing, and water remediation. By leveraging their fluorescence properties, CDs can sensitively detect microplastics in diverse environmental matrices. Additionally, their ability to interact with gas molecules makes them suitable for developing efficient gas sensors to monitor air pollutants. In water remediation, CDs can be utilized as adsorbents, photocatalysts, and electrochemical sensors to remove heavy metals, organic pollutants, and other contaminants. Despite significant advancements, several challenges persist. These include the need for scalable and environmentally friendly synthesis methods, improved selectivity and sensitivity, and long-term stability. Future research should focus on addressing these limitations and exploring innovative applications of CDs in environmental remediation. By harnessing the potential of CDs, we can contribute to a cleaner and more sustainable future.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Carbon dots (CDs) have emerged as a promising class of nanomaterials for addressing critical environmental challenges. Their unique optical, chemical, and electronic properties enable various applications, including microplastic detection, gas sensing, and water remediation. By leveraging their fluorescence properties, CDs can sensitively detect microplastics in diverse environmental matrices. Additionally, their ability to interact with gas molecules makes them suitable for developing efficient gas sensors to monitor air pollutants. In water remediation, CDs can be utilized as adsorbents, photocatalysts, and electrochemical sensors to remove heavy metals, organic pollutants, and other contaminants. Despite significant advancements, several challenges persist. These include the need for scalable and environmentally friendly synthesis methods, improved selectivity and sensitivity, and long-term stability. Future research should focus on addressing these limitations and exploring innovative applications of CDs in environmental remediation. By harnessing the potential of CDs, we can contribute to a cleaner and more sustainable future.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: Microplastic (MP) pollution has become a pervasive environmental issue, with concentrations ranging from 70.8 particles per m³ in marine environments to 0.28–4.18 particles per liter in wastewater effluents (Pourebrahimi & Pirooz, 2023; Kurniawan et al., 2023; Kumari et al., 2024). MPs, along with nanoplastics (NPs), originate from the degradation of larger plastics, posing risks to ecosystems, human health, and food safety through bioaccumulation and trophic transfer (Sutkar et al., 2023; Tammina et al., 2023; Zhang et al., 2024). Similarly, the annual average concentrations of NO₂ (33.91 ± 30.38 µg/m³), SO₂ (43.96 ± 20.12 µg/m³), PM2.5 (52.25 ± 55.26 µg/m³), and PM10 (77.58 ± 80.61 µg/m³) in urban areas reflect the growing threat of air pollution, which contributes to respiratory and cardiovascular diseases, climate change, and reduced agricultural productivity (Haque et al., 2024; Aragón et al., 2025; Ansari and Quaff, 2025). Additionally, water contamination with heavy metals, pesticides, and industrial pollutants adversely impacts aquatic ecosystems, soil quality, and human health (Rasheed, 2023; Tran et al., 2023; Kemarau et al., 2024). These environmental issues, though distinct, are deeply interconnected, as MPs can adsorb airborne pollutants and leach toxic substances into water bodies, while air pollution can exacerbate water quality degradation through acid rain (Chen et al., 2023; Latiza and Rubi, 2024; Latiza et al., 2024).

Addressing these interconnected challenges requires innovative solutions capable of tackling multiple pollutants simultaneously. Among the emerging technologies, carbon dots (CDs) have garnered attention for their exceptional properties, such as tunable fluorescence, high surface area, and functional group versatility (Hallaji et al., 2023; Qin et al., 2024; Wang et al., 2023). These properties enable CDs to detect, monitor, and remediate pollutants in diverse environmental settings. Recent studies include CDs facilitating early detection of MPs through fluorescence tagging, improve air quality monitoring via gas-sensing applications, and enhance water purification by adsorbing or catalytically degrading heavy metals and organic pollutants (Yu et al., 2024; Thakur et al., 2024; Hassan Ahmed & Soylak, 2024). Moreover, the synthesis of CDs from waste-derived precursors aligns with sustainable practices, making them an eco-friendly alternative to conventional materials (Lin et al., 2023; Zhang & Tong, 2024; Huang et al., 2024).

Despite their potential, significant challenges remain, including scalability issues, limited long-term stability under environmental conditions, and a need for comprehensive studies on their environmental safety (Kainth et al., 2023; Li et al., 2024; Soni et al., 2024). Understanding how CDs can interact with MPs during different stages of degradation, such as enhancing their detection or altering their chemical properties for easier removal, is still in its infancy and demands further investigation.

This review explores the environmental applications of CDs in combating the “triple threat” of MP, air and water pollution. It identifies research gaps, such as the scalability of CDs and their effectiveness across various environmental matrices, while also highlighting advancements in their use for pollutant detection and remediation. By contextualizing these innovations within the broader challenges of environmental sustainability, this study aims to provide a roadmap for leveraging CDs to develop effective, eco-friendly solutions for global environmental challenges. Table 1 provides a concise overview of recent studies showcasing the diverse applications of carbon dots in addressing these pressing environmental challenges.

CDs, or carbon nanodots, are a class of fluorescent nanomaterials typically smaller than 10 nm. Their structure often consists of stacked graphene layers interconnected by interlayer covalent bonds, with various surface functional groups that provide chemical versatility (Gong et al., 2024; Boukhvalov et al., 2024; Gorji et al., 2023). The optical properties of CDs, particularly their fluorescence emission, are highly tunable and can be precisely modulated by controlling their size, surface chemistry, and defect states. Emission colors are influenced by the number of layers, interlayer bonds, defects, and surface oxidation levels, which can be optimized to meet the demands of specific applications (Boukhvalov et al., 2024; Gorji et al., 2023; Jafarzadeh et al., 2024). Fluorescence quantum yield is improved in CDs synthesized via hydrothermal methods due to the effective passivation of surface states (Gong et al., 2024).

CDs demonstrate strong photoluminescence across a broad spectrum, and their fluorescence behavior is stable under UV exposure, high salt concentrations, and across a wide pH range. This makes them ideal for environmental monitoring, where robust performance is required under harsh conditions (Yadav et al., 2023; Bressi et al., 2023; Ma et al., 2024). Their fluorescence quenching behavior, which can occur through interactions with quenchers like nitrites or heavy metals, has been exploited for highly sensitive pollutant detection, such as sodium nitrite with a detection limit of 0.692 μM (Liu et al., 2024).

CDs are characterized by a high density of surface functional groups, including hydroxyl, carboxyl, amine, and other moieties introduced during or after synthesis (Yadav et al., 2023a; Bressi et al., 2023; Ma et al., 2024). These functional groups are critical for environmental applications as they influence the adsorption, interaction, and detection of pollutants. Hydroxyl and carboxyl groups of CDs enhance the adsorption of heavy metals like Pb2+ and Cd2+ through chelation and electrostatic interactions (Liu et al., 2024; Singh et al., 2023; Hussein Abdurahman et al., 2023). Amine groups improve selectivity in gas sensors for pollutants such as NH₃ and NO₂ by providing specific binding sites (Yadav et al., 2023a; Li et al., 2024). Surface functionalization with specific ligands has been shown to increase selectivity for MPs and NPs, enabling their fluorescence tagging and differentiation from other organic contaminants (Liu et al., 2024; Wang et al., 2023; Khan et al., 2023).

The introduction of nitrogen and sulfur doping during synthesis can further tailor surface chemistry, boosting adsorption capacity for organic pollutants or enhancing fluorescence quantum yield for sensor applications (Ghosh et al., 2023; Chen et al., 2024; Redondo-Fernandez et al., 2023). CDs doped with sulfur demonstrated enhanced performance in detecting polycyclic aromatic hydrocarbons due to stronger π-π interactions (Ozyurt et al., 2023).

The behavior of CDs in the presence of complex contaminant mixtures, including MPs, is a critical aspect of their environmental applicability. CDs exhibit selective fluorescence emission even in the presence of other fluorescent particles or compounds, such as polyaromatic hydrocarbons and some organic dyes (Seven et al., 2023). However, challenges remain in improving selectivity when contaminants have overlapping fluorescence spectra. Doping CDs with rare-earth metals or functionalizing their surface with specific ligands can significantly improve their ability to discriminate between MPs and other particles in mixed matrices (Rodríguez-Varillas et al., 2024; Bi et al., 2024; Ngo et al., 2023).

Moreover, the competitive adsorption of similar-sized organic and inorganic molecules can influence the efficacy of CDs in complex environments. Surface modifications with polymers or metal-organic frameworks (MOFs) have been employed to enhance selectivity and stability in these scenarios (Emam et al., 2023; Radha Krushna et al., 2024). For example, CDs embedded within MOF structures showed superior performance in adsorbing MPs while excluding non-target molecules (Oladzadabbasabadi et al., 2023).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Influence of synthesis methods on properties
ce:section.ce:para: The physical and chemical properties of CDs are strongly influenced by their synthesis method. Hydrothermal and solvothermal methods produce CDs with high quantum yields and uniform size distributions, while chemical oxidation methods result in CDs with abundant functional groups for adsorption and sensing applications (Gong et al., 2024; Liu et al., 2024). Waste-derived CDs, synthesized from precursors like spent coffee grounds or food waste, exhibit surface areas as high as 315.18 m2/g, which significantly enhances their adsorption capacity for pollutants (Singh et al., 2023; Lestari et al., 2023).

Key parameters in synthesis, such as reaction temperature, time, and precursor type, can fine-tune the properties of CDs for specific applications such as low-temperature synthesis favors surface functionalization, making CDs suitable for adsorption of heavy metals. High-temperature processes improve crystallinity, enhancing fluorescence properties for sensing applications. Post-synthesis modifications, such as doping or passivation, enable tailoring of surface properties to meet specific environmental needs (Ghosh et al., 2023; Redondo-Fernandez et al., 2023).

CDs are not only versatile but also sustainable, as they can be synthesized from renewable resources or waste materials. This aligns with circular economy principles and reduces the environmental impact of their production. CDs derived from agricultural waste have been applied to remove oil films from water, demonstrating both environmental and economic benefits. Their exceptional photostability ensures prolonged functionality under environmental conditions, supporting sustainable applications. Combined with their biocompatibility, CDs represent a promising solution for real-world applications, including detecting MPs in wastewater, monitoring air quality, and remediating heavy metal contamination in water (Yadav et al., 2023; Li et al., 2023).

The application of CDs in MP detection via fluorescence sensing represents a burgeoning field with immense potential. The unique optical properties of CDs, such as high fluorescence quantum yield, tunable emission, and excellent photostability, make them ideal for labeling and tracking MPs in various environmental matrices (Zhang & Tong, 2024; Liu et al., 2024; Lestari et al., 2023), as shown in Fig. 1 . These properties enable CDs to selectively label and detect specific MPs, such as polyethylene, through fluorescence turn-on or quenching mechanisms (Li et al., 2023; Thacharodi et al., 2024). Moreover, innovative approaches like paper-based analytical devices incorporating CDs have shown promise for rapid and cost-effective MP quantification in water, achieving detection limits as low as 3.82 μM for polyethylene MPs (Li et al., 2023).

The fluorescence mechanism of CDs, particularly the fluorescence turn-on/turn-off phenomenon, is influenced by factors such as surface functional groups, electronic interactions, and quenching agents. Fluorescence quenching typically occurs in the presence of specific MPs due to electron or energy transfer processes between CDs and the MP surface. Conversely, fluorescence intensification can result from adsorption of MPs, which alters the surface energy states of CDs. For instance, fluorescence turn-on can occur through the removal of quenching agents or the introduction of specific MPs that enhance the radiative recombination of excitons. The detailed understanding of these mechanisms is critical for optimizing MP detection sensitivity and specificity (Laddha et al., 2023; Nawaz et al., 2024; Rajesh & Saravanakumar, 2023).

Experimental conditions, such as pH, temperature, and the nature of the doping material, significantly impact fluorescence behavior and must be optimized for reliable MP detection. For example, sulfur doping introduces new energy levels that enhance fluorescence intensity, whereas nitrogen doping improves the quantum yield of CDs (Li et al., 2023; Liu et al., 2024; Feng et al., 2024; Zhang et al., 2022). In Table 2 , the influence of these parameters is summarized, but further explanations are warranted to elucidate their effects on MP detection performance.

Pyrene-derived CDs doped with sulfur demonstrate enhanced detection sensitivity due to improved fluorescence intensity and energy transfer efficiency. Sulfur doping facilitates specific interactions with polyethylene (PE) MPs, enabling a fluorescence turn-on mechanism at elevated temperatures (Li et al., 2023). Shrub leaves, as a natural carbon source, produce CDs with excellent environmental compatibility, while their fluorescence properties are stable near neutral pH, making them suitable for real-world water samples (Liu et al., 2024). Phenylguanidine carbonate-derived CDs offer versatility in detecting a variety of MPs due to their broad fluorescence range, although the lack of doping material limits specificity (Feng et al., 2024). Boron doping in pyrene-derived CDs enhances fluorescence emission, enabling the differentiation of multiple MP types based on distinct optical signatures (Zhang et al., 2022).

CD-based detection of MPs offers distinct advantages over traditional methods like FTIR and Raman spectroscopy. FTIR and Raman techniques provide molecular fingerprints of MPs, making them highly reliable for chemical identification. However, they require extensive sample preparation, have limitations with low MP concentrations, and are less effective for in situ detection. CDs, in contrast, enable rapid, cost-effective, and highly sensitive detection with minimal sample preparation (Hayder et al., 2025). Additionally, CDs provide the potential for real-time monitoring and imaging due to their fluorescence properties (Rani et al., 2023; Mohandoss et al., 2023; Sohal et al., 2023; Zhou et al., 2023). However, CDs may face challenges in distinguishing MPs from other organic and inorganic compounds that also fluoresce, potentially impacting selectivity.

Interference from other particles or contaminants, such as dissolved organic matter, can alter the fluorescence response, reducing selectivity and accuracy (Li et al., 2023; Liu et al., 2024; Algethami and Abdelhamid, 2024; Nohara et al., 2024; Paiman et al., 2023; Baumgarten et al., 2023). The separation of CDs from MPs after detection remains a logistical hurdle, particularly in large-scale applications. Techniques such as magnetic separation or functionalized filters may address this limitation. Adapting CD-based techniques for field-scale applications requires robust methods for stabilizing CDs under varying environmental conditions, such as high salinity or extreme pH ranges (Cortés-Arriagada et al., 2023; Panigrahi et al., 2023; Sun et al., 2024; Durrani et al., 2023). Focusing on improving the selectivity of CDs by tailoring their surface functional groups for specific MP interactions and exploring hybrid detection methods that combine CDs with advanced imaging or spectroscopic techniques is the current direction. Additionally, incorporating CDs into scalable devices, such as portable sensors, could revolutionize MP detection in real-world scenarios (Niu et al., 2023; Camilli et al., 2024; Cheng et al., 2025).

CDs have unique properties that make them very well-suited for gas sensing. The various surface functional groups on CDs allow for easy modification, enabling them to interact selectively with specific target gases. This interaction often leads to detectable changes in the properties of CD (Abd-Elkader et al., 2023; Crispi et al., 2023; Song et al., 2023), as shown in Fig. 2 . CDs can interact with target gases can cause different optical changes, demonstrating their flexibility for various gas sensing needs (Cai et al., 2023; Mondal et al., 2023; Zhang et al., 2023). The use of CDs in gas sensing has become a promising area due to their unique properties and simple synthesis methods (Seekaew et al., 2023; Wang et al., 2024), as shown in Table 3 .

The interaction between CDs and gas molecules is typically associated with charge transfer, surface adsorption, or molecular quenching/enhancement mechanisms. The adsorption of gases like NO2 or triethylamine onto CD surfaces can induce electron transfer, altering their fluorescence intensity or conductivity (Seekaew et al., 2023; Yang et al., 2023; Thota et al., 2022). Fluorescence quenching often occurs through static quenching (complex formation) or dynamic quenching (collisional interactions), depending on the target gas and environmental conditions. Similarly, fluorescence enhancement may result from energy transfer between CDs and specific analytes. The optical shifts in CDs are tied directly to their surface functionalization, highlighting the importance of controlled synthesis and doping in tuning their performance (Yin et al., 2024).

Sensitivity, a measure of the ability of the sensor to detect target gases at low concentrations, is reported to be high across most entries in Table 3, reflecting the superior interaction of CDs with gas molecules due to their large surface area and rich surface functional groups (Kanzariya et al., 2024; Sawalha et al., 2021; Yang et al., 2023). This high sensitivity is particularly evident for H2S and triethylamine, where LODs of 200 ppb and 46 ppb, respectively, are achieved (Thota et al., 2022; Zhang et al., 2021). Such performance is vital for early detection of harmful gases in industrial and environmental monitoring.

Response and recovery times, which indicate how quickly the sensor reacts to the presence and absence of target gases, vary significantly across studies. Sensors targeting NO2 showed relatively slow response and recovery times of 150 s and 315 s, respectively, which may limit their use in real-time applications (Sawalha et al., 2021). In contrast, sensors for triethylamine demonstrated much faster dynamics, with response and recovery times as low as 16 s and 90 s, respectively (Yang et al., 2023). These differences can be attributed to factors such as the physicochemical properties of the target gases, the surface chemistry of the CDs, and the incorporation of dopants or modifiers like indium or tin oxide (SnO2), which enhance adsorption and desorption kinetics (Yang et al., 2023; Yin et al., 2024). Faster response times are particularly desirable for applications requiring real-time monitoring, such as workplace safety and environmental hazard detection.

Stability and repeatability are crucial metrics for ensuring long-term and consistent performance. Most sensors in the table are reported to have good stability, likely due to the inherent robustness of CDs and the protective effects of dopants like zinc oxide (ZnO) and titanium dioxide (TiO2) (Seekaew et al., 2023; Yin et al., 2024; Liu et al., 2023). However, stability under varying environmental conditions, such as extreme temperatures or the presence of interfering substances, requires further exploration. Enhancing stability through encapsulation or surface modifications may be necessary for practical deployment in complex environments (Thota et al., 2022).

The limit of detection (LOD) is another critical parameter, with values ranging from sub-ppm to ppb levels across studies. For example, sensors detecting CNCl and formaldehyde achieved impressive LODs of 0.5 ppm or lower, demonstrating their capability to identify trace levels of hazardous gases (Kanzariya et al., 2024; Liu et al., 2023). Such low LODs are attributed to the high quantum yield and effective charge transfer properties of CDs, which amplify the sensing signal even at minimal gas concentrations (Thota et al., 2022). These findings underscore the potential of CDs in applications requiring extreme sensitivity, such as healthcare diagnostics and environmental pollution monitoring.

The role of doping and raw material selection is a recurring theme in Table 3. Doping with elements like indium, chlorine, or nickel significantly enhances sensitivity and selectivity by introducing electronic and chemical modifications to the CD surface (Kanzariya et al., 2024; Yang et al., 2023; Thota et al., 2022). For instance, the addition of SnO2 to CDs increases sensitivity towards triethylamine, likely by improving charge transfer and facilitating gas adsorption through synergistic interactions (Yin et al., 2024). Similarly, using biomass-derived CDs, such as those from marine polysaccharides or sucralose, offers an eco-friendly and cost-effective alternative for producing high-performance gas sensors (Thota et al., 2022; Zhang et al., 2021).

Despite these promising results, several limitations and knowledge gaps are evident in Table 3. While many studies report good stability, comprehensive assessments of sensor longevity, especially under harsh conditions, are lacking. The response and recovery times for some gases, such as NO2 and ethanol, remain relatively slow, necessitating further research into enhancing desorption kinetics and reducing sensor lag (Sawalha et al., 2021; Hejazi et al., 2024).

While CDs exhibit superior performance characteristics, challenges persist in adapting these sensors for real-world applications. Issues like interference from co-existing gases, stability under fluctuating conditions, and the scalability of CD production need to be addressed. Additionally, separating CDs from the analytes post-detection and integrating CD-based sensors into commercial devices remain hurdles for large-scale adoption (Yang et al., 2023; Thota et al., 2022). The direction is developing hybrid materials combining CDs with other nanomaterials to enhance selectivity and durability. Exploring green synthesis approaches to reduce production costs and environmental impact. Optimizing sensor designs for industrial and environmental applications with a focus on multi-analyte detection.

Hazardous pollutants, such as heavy metals, organic dyes, and pesticides, pose significant threats to aquatic ecosystems and human health, necessitating effective remediation strategies (Kumari et al., 2024; Hebbar et al., 2023; Kaur et al., 2023), as illustrated in Fig. 3 . CDs have emerged as a versatile tool for environmental remediation due to their unique physicochemical properties, including high surface area, strong adsorption capabilities, and remarkable photocatalytic activity (Soni et al., 2024; Rangel et al., 2023). Upon light irradiation, CDs generate reactive oxygen species (ROS), such as hydroxyl radicals (-OH) and superoxide anions (O2-), which are highly reactive and can degrade pollutants into non-toxic byproducts (Rangel et al., 2023; Gallareta-Olivares et al., 2023). Moreover, the strong interaction between the surface functional groups of CDs (e.g., hydroxyl, carboxyl, or amine groups) and pollutants allows for efficient adsorption, as summarized in Table 4 .

Adsorption is a prominent mechanism where pollutants are physically or chemically bound to the surface of CDs or their composites. This interaction is primarily driven by van der Waals forces, hydrogen bonding, electrostatic attraction, and coordination interactions. The adsorption of methyl violet dye onto a sodium alginate-based CD-nanomagnetic composite is enhanced by electrostatic interactions between the positively charged dye and the negatively charged CD surface (Momina & Ahmad, 2024). Similarly, mercury ions (Hg2+) are adsorbed onto functionalized dendritic silica doped with red-emissive CDs through coordination interactions between mercury ions and surface ligands like amine or thiol groups (Yadav et al., 2023). The adsorption efficiency is influenced by factors such as pH, ionic strength, and pollutant concentration. Optimal conditions, such as a pH of 6 for methyl violet and a pH range of 5–8 for mercury ions, ensure maximum interaction with the CD surface while minimizing competition from other ions (Momina & Ahmad, 2024; Yadav et al., 2023).

Photodegradation utilizes the photocatalytic properties of CDs to degrade pollutants under light irradiation. When exposed to UV or visible light, CDs absorb photons, promoting electrons from the valence band to the conduction band and creating electron-hole pairs. These pairs interact with water or oxygen molecules, generating ROS that break down pollutants into less harmful products. The photocatalytic degradation of Rhodamine B dye by sulfur- and nitrogen-doped CDs (S,N-CQDs) is facilitated by enhanced light absorption and charge separation due to the dopants (Rahmani et al., 2024). Similarly, CQDs-modified reduced ultrathin g-C3N4 generates a high density of ROS for the degradation of pharmaceuticals and personal care products at an acidic pH of 3, which accelerates the reaction kinetics (Jin et al., 2021).

Fluorescence sensing is another innovative approach where the interaction between CDs and specific pollutants induces changes in fluorescence intensity. Chlortetracycline can quench the fluorescence of CDs covalently bonded to tungsten disulfide, allowing for its detection and removal (Long et al., 2022). This mechanism is sensitive to changes in the electronic environment of CDs, making it effective for trace pollutant detection.

Different types of CDs exhibit unique chemical and optical properties that make them suitable for specific pollutants. For example, nitrogen- and sulfur-doped CDs are particularly effective for photocatalytic applications due to their ability to extend light absorption into the visible range, as demonstrated in the degradation of Rhodamine B dye (Rahmani et al., 2024). Similarly, CDs derived from biomass sources, such as apple peel or Iranian Artemisia dracunculus, are cost-effective and environmentally friendly while exhibiting high adsorption capacity for dyes like methyl violet and malachite green (Momina & Ahmad, 2024; Montazer et al., 2023). For heavy metals like mercury, CDs functionalized with thiol or amine groups offer strong coordination interactions, enhancing removal efficiency (Yadav et al., 2023).

Although CDs are generally considered biocompatible, potential toxicity risks should not be overlooked. Studies indicate that the toxicity of CDs depends on factors such as surface functional groups, size, and the presence of dopants (Macawile et al., 2022; Mahajan et al., 2023; Bhattacharjee et al., 2023). Nitrogen-doped CDs may release reactive intermediates that pose risks to human health at high concentrations. However, preliminary research shows that CDs synthesized from natural or food-grade sources exhibit low cytotoxicity, making them safer alternatives for environmental applications (Aygun et al., 2024). Further toxicological studies are essential to fully understand their impact on human health and ecosystems.

CDs offer distinct advantages over conventional remediation technologies, such as activated carbon or metal oxide photocatalysts, due to their low cost, tunable properties, and ability to operate under visible light. However, activated carbon typically excels in adsorptive capacity for non-polar pollutants, while metal oxides like TiO2 are often more stable under extreme conditions (Lathe & Palve, 2023]. CDs are most advantageous in applications requiring multifunctionality, such as simultaneous pollutant detection and degradation. For example, their fluorescence sensing capabilities set them apart from traditional photocatalysts, which lack detection functionality. To optimize applicability, researchers should consider hybrid systems combining CDs with conventional materials, leveraging the strengths of each technology.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Challenges on the utilization of carbon dots
ce:section.ce:para: Despite the applications, as shown on the Fig. 4 below and its promising potential of CDs in various fields, several challenges need to be addressed to realize their full potential. In MP detection, the fluorescence emission of CDs can be influenced by environmental factors like pH, ionic strength, and the presence of natural organic matter, potentially leading to inaccurate results (Feng et al., 2024). The interaction between CDs and MPs is further complicated by the varying size, shape, and surface properties of MPs, making consistent and reliable detection across different types challenging (Mahajan et al., 2023; Zhang et al., 2022). Additionally, the separation and purification of CDs from complex environmental samples can be cumbersome, hindering their practical application (Mahajan et al., 2023).

In gas sensing applications, the sensitivity and selectivity of CD-based sensors can be influenced by environmental factors such as humidity and temperature (Seekaew et al., 2023; Thota et al., 2022; Yin et al., 2024; Liu et al., 2023), necessitating the development of more robust sensing mechanisms. Long-term stability and reproducibility also require improvement for real-world applications (Sawalha et al., 2021; Thota et al., 2022; Yin et al., 2024; Liu et al., 2023). While integrating CDs with other nanomaterials can enhance sensing capabilities, it also complicates the fabrication process and can lead to compatibility issues (Seekaew et al., 2023; Yang et al., 2023; Hejazi et al., 2024; Yin et al., 2024).

In environmental remediation, the tendency of CDs to aggregate can lead to reduced fluorescence and adsorption capacity, requiring strategies like surface passivation or immobilization. The excitation-dependent photoluminescence of many CDs can limit their sensitivity and selectivity, necessitating the development of excitation-independent or red-emissive CDs (Yadav et al., 2023). The photocatalytic efficiency of CDs can be hindered by factors such as fast recombination of photogenerated electron-hole pairs and limited light absorption, often requiring the formation of composites or doping with heteroatoms (Rahmani et al., 2024; Jin et al., 2021). The potential toxicity and environmental impact of CDs, particularly those synthesized from non-renewable or chemically intensive precursors, require careful evaluation (Kamel et al., 2024). Additionally, the scalability and cost-effectiveness of CD production and their integration into existing water treatment technologies pose challenges for practical implementation (Momina & Ahmad, 2024). Finally, the long-term stability and reusability of CDs in real-world environmental conditions require further investigation and optimization (Long et al., 2022).

Despite the promising potential of CDs in various fields, several challenges need to be addressed to realize their full potential. These challenges are diverse and span applications such as microplastic (MP) detection, gas sensing, and environmental remediation, requiring a comprehensive and systematic approach for their resolution.

In the context of MP detection, environmental factors such as pH, ionic strength, and the presence of natural organic matter can significantly influence the fluorescence emission of CDs, potentially leading to inaccurate results (Feng et al., 2024). For instance, changes in pH can alter the surface charge of CDs, disrupting their interaction with MPs, while organic matter can quench fluorescence, reducing sensitivity. Furthermore, MPs vary greatly in size, shape, and surface properties, complicating the consistent and reliable detection of diverse MP types. This variability necessitates the development of tailored CD formulations or multifunctional CDs that can detect a broad spectrum of MPs without compromising selectivity or sensitivity (Zhang et al., 2022; Macawile et al., 2022). Additionally, the separation and purification of CDs from complex environmental samples can be cumbersome, requiring advanced methods such as magnetic separation or membrane-based filtration to enhance their practicality (Mahajan et al., 2023).

In gas sensing applications, CDs face challenges in maintaining sensitivity and selectivity under fluctuating environmental conditions. Humidity, for instance, can interfere with gas adsorption on the CD surface, while temperature variations can alter the photophysical properties of CDs, affecting sensor reliability (Seekaew et al., 2023; Thota et al., 2022; Yin et al., 2024; Liu et al., 2023). Ensuring long-term stability and reproducibility is critical, as sensor performance can degrade over time due to material fatigue or environmental exposure. While integrating CDs with other nanomaterials, such as metal oxides or graphene, has been shown to enhance sensing capabilities, these hybrid materials introduce complexities in fabrication, compatibility, and scaling up (Seekaew et al., 2023; Yang et al., 2023; Hejazi et al., 2024; Yin et al., 2024). Further research is required to design robust sensing mechanisms that maintain performance under real-world conditions.

In environmental remediation, one of the primary challenges is the tendency of CDs to aggregate, which can reduce their surface area and adsorption capacity. This aggregation can also lead to fluorescence quenching, diminishing their effectiveness in photodegradation or sensing applications. Surface passivation or immobilization on substrates like silica or polymers can mitigate these effects, but these methods may complicate scalability. The excitation-dependent photoluminescence of many CDs, while useful for tunable applications, can limit sensitivity and selectivity in specific remediation tasks, especially under complex environmental conditions. Developing excitation-independent or red-emissive CDs is a promising direction to address this limitation (Yadav et al., 2023). Moreover, the photocatalytic efficiency of CDs is often hindered by the fast recombination of photogenerated electron-hole pairs and limited light absorption in the visible range. These issues can be partially overcome by forming composites with materials like TiO2 or doping with heteroatoms such as nitrogen and sulfur, which enhance charge separation and broaden light absorption (Rahmani et al., 2024; Jin et al., 2021). However, these modifications must be optimized to maintain cost-effectiveness and environmental sustainability.

The potential toxicity and environmental impact of CDs synthesized from non-renewable or chemically intensive precursors also warrant careful consideration. While CDs are generally considered biocompatible, studies have shown that their toxicity can vary depending on surface functionalization, size, and the type of precursors used (Kamel et al., 2024). For instance, CDs with high concentrations of heavy metals or residual synthetic chemicals can pose risks to aquatic ecosystems and human health. Ensuring the use of renewable precursors, such as agricultural waste or plant-derived materials, alongside rigorous toxicity testing, is essential for sustainable development.

Scalability and cost-effectiveness remain significant barriers to integrating CDs into practical water treatment technologies. While methods like microwave-assisted synthesis or hydrothermal approaches have improved production efficiency, achieving large-scale production with consistent quality is still challenging (Momina & Ahmad, 2024). Additionally, the long-term stability and reusability of CDs under real-world environmental conditions are often overlooked in laboratory studies. Testing in diverse and dynamic environmental scenarios is needed to ensure reliability over extended periods (Long et al., 2022).

To address these challenges systematically, a dedicated section exploring limitations and potential solutions are recommended. This includes identifying strategies for improving fluorescence stability in MP detection, developing robust gas sensing mechanisms, and enhancing photocatalytic and adsorption efficiencies in environmental remediation. Incorporating renewable precursors, optimizing synthesis methods for scalability, and conducting comprehensive toxicity assessments will ensure CDs are both effective and environmentally sustainable.

CDs are a promising and versatile nanomaterial with significant potential for environmental applications, including MP detection, gas sensing, and environmental remediation. Their unique properties such as tunable fluorescence, high photostability, and biocompatibility make them attractive for addressing pollution. However, challenges such as environmental interference, aggregation, and scalability remain, limiting their widespread use. Key limitations include interference from complex environmental matrices, aggregation issues, and the lack of scalable synthesis methods. Future research should focus on overcoming these challenges by developing excitation-independent CDs, optimizing sustainable synthesis processes, and exploring hybrid systems for enhanced performance. Long-term studies on the stability and environmental impact of CDs will also be critical for their successful integration into real-world applications. While CDs offer great potential, continued innovation is needed to unlock their full capabilities and ensure their practical implementation in addressing global environmental challenges.
